Intro to CS - Python.teks_description: Choose, identify, and use the appropriate data types for program variables
Intro to CS - Python.prompt: This topic covers Python fundamentals for Intro to CS - Python students (TEKS §126.33.c.6N). Students should identify and use appropriate data types: int (whole numbers), float (decimal numbers), str (text strings), bool (True/False), and NoneType. Key concepts: type() function, type casting (int(), float(), str()), dynamic typing in Python, string vs. numeric types, and choosing the right type for the task (e.g., int for counting, float for measurement). Vocabulary: data type, integer, float, string, boolean, type casting, dynamic typing, literal, expression. Students should write syntactically correct Python including proper indentation, colons, and quotation marks. Common errors: mixing tabs and spaces, forgetting colons after if/for/def, type mismatch in operations (e.g., '5' + 3), and case sensitivity.

Intro to CS - Python.teks_description: Analyze the concept of a variable and use variables in programs
Intro to CS - Python.prompt: This topic covers variables and operators for Intro to CS - Python students (TEKS §126.33.c.6O). Students should create meaningful variable names following Python conventions (snake_case), understand assignment (=) vs. equality (==), and use arithmetic (+, -, *, /, //, %, **), comparison (<, >, <=, >=, ==, !=), logical (and, or, not), and assignment operators (+=, -=, *=). Key concepts: variable as a named reference to a value, reassignment, multiple assignment, and operator precedence. Vocabulary: variable, assignment, operator, operand, expression, precedence, modulo, floor division, increment. Common errors: using = instead of ==, integer vs. float division confusion (/ vs. //), and uninitialized variable errors.

Intro to CS - Python.teks_description: Develop algorithms to decision-making problems using branching control structures
Intro to CS - Python.prompt: This topic covers branching and looping for Intro to CS - Python students (TEKS §126.33.c.4R). Students should use if/elif/else for decision-making and for/while loops for repetition. Key concepts: boolean conditions, nested conditionals, comparison and logical operators in conditions, for loops with range() and iterables, while loops with sentinel values, break and continue statements, and avoiding infinite loops. Vocabulary: conditional, branch, loop, iteration, boolean, sentinel, accumulator, counter, infinite loop, nested. Students should trace through code predicting output. Common errors: incorrect indentation, off-by-one errors in range(), infinite while loops, and missing else/elif branches.

Intro to CS - Python.teks_description: Create subroutines that do not return values; §126.33.c.6(H) create subroutines that return typed values
Intro to CS - Python.prompt: This topic covers defining and using functions for Intro to CS - Python students (TEKS §126.33.c.6G, §126.33.c.6H). Students should create functions using def, understand parameters vs. arguments, return values vs. printing, and scope (local vs. global variables). Key concepts: function definition, calling functions, positional and keyword arguments, default parameter values, return statement, None return (void functions), docstrings, and the DRY principle (Don't Repeat Yourself). Vocabulary: function, parameter, argument, return value, scope, local variable, global variable, call, define, docstring. Common errors: forgetting return (function returns None), modifying global variables unintentionally, and confusing print with return.

Intro to CS - Python.teks_description: Identify and use the structured data type of one-dimensional arrays
Intro to CS - Python.prompt: This topic covers sequence data structures for Intro to CS - Python students (TEKS §126.33.c.6Q). Students should create and manipulate lists (mutable) and tuples (immutable). Key operations: indexing (0-based), slicing [start:stop:step], append(), insert(), remove(), pop(), len(), sort(), sorted(), and list comprehensions. Key concepts: mutability vs. immutability, iterating with for loops, nested lists, list methods vs. functions, and common patterns (accumulator, filter, map). Vocabulary: list, tuple, index, slice, mutable, immutable, append, iterate, comprehension, nested. Common errors: index out of range, modifying a list while iterating, confusing list methods with string methods, and off-by-one in slicing.

Intro to CS - Python.teks_description: Identify and use structured data types for organizing and accessing data
Intro to CS - Python.prompt: This topic covers dictionary data structures for Intro to CS - Python students (TEKS §126.33.c.6Q). Students should create and use dictionaries (key-value pairs), access values by key, add/update/delete entries, and iterate over keys, values, and items. Key concepts: keys must be immutable (strings, numbers, tuples), values can be any type, dict methods (.get(), .keys(), .values(), .items()), checking membership with 'in', nested dictionaries, and dictionary comprehensions. Vocabulary: dictionary, key, value, key-value pair, mapping, hash, lookup, nested dictionary. Applications: frequency counting, lookup tables, JSON-like data. Common errors: KeyError from accessing nonexistent keys (use .get()), mutable keys, and confusing list indices with dictionary keys.

Intro to CS - Python.teks_description: Represent and manipulate text data in programs
Intro to CS - Python.prompt: This topic covers text processing for Intro to CS - Python students (TEKS §126.33.c.6P). Students should manipulate strings using indexing, slicing, concatenation (+), repetition (*), and string methods. Key methods: .upper(), .lower(), .strip(), .split(), .join(), .find(), .replace(), .startswith(), .endswith(), .format(), and f-strings. Key concepts: strings are immutable sequences, escape characters (\n, \t, \\), string formatting (f-strings and .format()), and iterating over characters. Vocabulary: string, immutable, concatenation, slice, escape character, f-string, method, format, parse. Common errors: treating strings as mutable, off-by-one in slicing, forgetting that methods return new strings, and escape character mistakes.

Intro to CS - Python.teks_description: Create and properly label and display output; handle file-based input/output
Intro to CS - Python.prompt: This topic covers file handling for Intro to CS - Python students (TEKS §126.33.c.3A). Students should read from and write to text files using open(), read(), readline(), readlines(), write(), and the 'with' statement for automatic file closing. Key concepts: file modes ('r', 'w', 'a', 'r+'), the with statement as a context manager, reading line by line vs. entire file, stripping newline characters, handling file paths, and CSV file basics. Vocabulary: file, open, close, read, write, append, context manager, mode, path, delimiter, CSV. Common errors: forgetting to close files (use 'with'), writing to a file opened in read mode, overwriting files with 'w' mode, and not stripping newlines.

Intro to CS - Python.teks_description: Identify reusable components from existing code including modules and libraries
Intro to CS - Python.prompt: This topic covers Python modules and imports for Intro to CS - Python students (TEKS §126.33.c.4E). Students should import and use standard library modules (math, random, os, sys, datetime) and understand module organization. Key concepts: import statements (import module, from module import function, import module as alias), the standard library, installing third-party packages with pip, creating your own modules, and the __name__ == '__main__' pattern. Vocabulary: module, package, import, library, standard library, pip, namespace, alias. Common errors: circular imports, name conflicts (shadowing built-in names), and importing everything with 'from module import *' (namespace pollution).

Intro to CS - Python.teks_description: Debug and solve problems using error messages
Intro to CS - Python.prompt: This topic covers finding and fixing errors for Intro to CS - Python students (TEKS §126.33.c.4J). Students should identify and resolve syntax errors, runtime errors, and logic errors using error messages and debugging tools. Key concepts: reading traceback messages, common error types (SyntaxError, TypeError, NameError, IndexError, ValueError, KeyError), using print statements for debugging, Python debugger (pdb), breakpoints, and systematic debugging strategies (binary search, rubber duck). Vocabulary: bug, debug, syntax error, runtime error, logic error, traceback, exception, breakpoint, step through, stack trace. Common errors: ignoring error messages, fixing symptoms instead of root causes, and not testing edge cases.

Intro to CS - Python.teks_description: Create and implement common algorithms including searching and sorting
Intro to CS - Python.prompt: This topic covers fundamental algorithms for Intro to CS - Python students (TEKS §126.33.c.4K). Students should implement and analyze: linear search (O(n)), binary search (O(log n) on sorted data), bubble sort, selection sort, and insertion sort (all O(n^2)). Key concepts: algorithm efficiency (Big-O notation at introductory level), comparing search/sort approaches, when to use each algorithm, tracing through algorithms step by step, and understanding that Python's built-in sort uses Timsort (O(n log n)). Vocabulary: algorithm, search, sort, linear search, binary search, bubble sort, selection sort, insertion sort, efficiency, comparison. Common errors: applying binary search to unsorted data, off-by-one errors in search bounds, and infinite loops in sort implementations.

Intro to CS - Python.teks_description: Identify and use concepts of object-oriented design
Intro to CS - Python.prompt: This topic covers OOP fundamentals for Intro to CS - Python students (TEKS §126.33.c.6D). Students should define classes with __init__, instance methods, and instance variables, create objects, and understand encapsulation. Key concepts: class as a blueprint, object as an instance, self parameter, constructor (__init__), instance variables vs. class variables, methods, encapsulation (bundling data and behavior), and basic inheritance. Vocabulary: class, object, instance, constructor, method, attribute, self, encapsulation, inheritance, instantiation. Common errors: forgetting self parameter, confusing class variables with instance variables, and not calling __init__ properly.

Intro to CS - Python.teks_description: Create program solutions to problems using available mathematics library and external libraries
Intro to CS - Python.prompt: This topic covers using external libraries for Intro to CS - Python students (TEKS §126.33.c.4O). Students should use the math library (sqrt, pi, sin, cos, ceil, floor), random library (randint, choice, shuffle, random), and explore other libraries. Key concepts: importing specific functions vs. whole modules, reading library documentation, understanding function signatures, and leveraging libraries to avoid reinventing solutions. Vocabulary: library, module, function, documentation, API, dependency, install, pip, virtual environment. Students should solve problems using appropriate library functions rather than writing everything from scratch. Common errors: not importing before use, using wrong function names, and version compatibility issues.

Intro to CS - Python.teks_description: Create solutions addressing basic error handling
Intro to CS - Python.prompt: This topic covers exception handling for Intro to CS - Python students (TEKS §126.33.c.4L). Students should use try/except blocks to handle runtime errors gracefully, use specific exception types, and understand the try/except/else/finally structure. Key concepts: common exceptions (ValueError, TypeError, FileNotFoundError, ZeroDivisionError, IndexError), catching specific vs. general exceptions, raising exceptions with raise, and designing programs that handle errors without crashing. Vocabulary: exception, try, except, finally, raise, catch, handle, graceful degradation, traceback. Common errors: catching too broad exceptions (bare except), swallowing errors silently, and using exceptions for normal flow control.

Intro to CS - Python.teks_description: Identify and use the structured data type of one-dimensional arrays and basic data structures
Intro to CS - Python.prompt: This topic covers fundamental data structures for Intro to CS - Python students (TEKS §126.33.c.6Q). Students should understand and use lists, tuples, dictionaries, sets, and stacks/queues (using lists or collections.deque). Key concepts: choosing the right data structure for the task, time complexity of common operations (list access O(1), dict lookup O(1), list search O(n)), sets for unique elements and membership testing, and stack (LIFO) vs. queue (FIFO) behavior. Vocabulary: data structure, list, tuple, dictionary, set, stack, queue, LIFO, FIFO, deque, hash table. Common errors: using a list when a set would be more efficient, O(n) list operations in loops creating O(n^2), and unhashable type errors with sets.

Intro to CS - Python.teks_description: Create interactive input interfaces with relevant user prompts
Intro to CS - Python.prompt: This topic covers interactive input handling for Intro to CS - Python students (TEKS §126.33.c.3B). Students should use input() to get user data, provide clear prompts, validate input, and convert input types appropriately. Key concepts: input() always returns a string, type conversion (int(), float()), input validation loops (keep asking until valid), providing meaningful prompts, and handling unexpected input gracefully with try/except. Vocabulary: input, prompt, validation, type conversion, interactive, user interface, command line, stdin. Common errors: forgetting to convert input from string, not validating user input (crashing on bad input), and assuming users will enter exactly what's expected.

Intro to CS - Python.teks_description: Design a solution to a problem; §126.33.c.4(G) code a solution from a program design
Intro to CS - Python.prompt: This topic covers creating simple games for Intro to CS - Python students (TEKS §126.33.c.4F, §126.33.c.4G). Students should design and code text-based and simple graphical games using programming fundamentals. Key concepts: game loop (while running: get input, update state, display), state management with variables, random events, win/lose conditions, and user interaction. Projects: number guessing game, rock-paper-scissors, text adventure, tic-tac-toe, hangman. Vocabulary: game loop, state, event, condition, random, score, turn, player, animation, sprite. Common errors: game loop logic errors, not updating display after state changes, and missing edge cases in win conditions.

Intro to CS - Python.teks_description: Display simple vector graphics using lines, circles, and rectangles
Intro to CS - Python.prompt: This topic covers creating visual representations of data for Intro to CS - Python students (TEKS §126.33.c.3E). Students should use libraries like matplotlib and turtle to create charts, graphs, and simple graphics. Key concepts: plotting with matplotlib (plt.plot(), plt.bar(), plt.scatter(), plt.hist()), labeling axes and titles, choosing appropriate chart types, turtle graphics for drawing shapes and patterns. Vocabulary: plot, chart, graph, axis, label, title, legend, bar chart, scatter plot, histogram, turtle graphics, pixel. Common errors: unlabeled axes, choosing inappropriate chart types, scale issues, and not calling plt.show().

Intro to CS - Python.skill: Intro to Web Development with Python
Intro to CS - Python.teks_description: Design a solution to a problem using appropriate programming constructs
Intro to CS - Python.prompt: This topic covers basic web development concepts using Python for Intro to CS - Python students (TEKS §126.33.c.4F). Students should understand client-server architecture, create simple web applications using frameworks like Flask, and handle basic HTTP requests. Key concepts: HTML basics (tags, structure), HTTP request/response cycle, routes and URL mapping, templates, serving static content, and form handling. Vocabulary: web server, client, HTTP, GET, POST, route, template, HTML, URL, request, response, framework, Flask. Common errors: not understanding the request-response cycle, template syntax errors, and port conflicts when running local servers.

Intro to CS - Python.teks_description: Demonstrate proper digital etiquette, responsible use of software and version management
Intro to CS - Python.prompt: This topic covers Git fundamentals for Intro to CS - Python students (TEKS §126.33.c.5C). Students should use Git for tracking code changes and collaborating. Key commands: git init, git add, git commit, git status, git log, git diff, git branch, git checkout, git merge, git clone, git push, git pull. Key concepts: repository, staging area, commits as snapshots, branches for parallel development, merging, resolving conflicts, and remote repositories (GitHub). Vocabulary: repository, commit, stage, branch, merge, clone, push, pull, remote, conflict, diff, log. Common errors: committing sensitive files, merge conflicts from not pulling first, and unclear commit messages.

Computer programming.skill: Data Structures (Arrays, Linked Lists, Trees, Graphs)
Computer programming.teks_description: Computer Science II: Apply data structures including arrays, lists, stacks, queues, trees, and graphs
Computer programming.prompt: This topic covers advanced data structures for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.34.c.5). Students should implement and analyze arrays, linked lists (singly/doubly), stacks, queues, binary trees, binary search trees, heaps, hash tables, and graphs. Key concepts: time/space complexity of operations (insert, delete, search, access), choosing appropriate structures for different problems, tree traversals (inorder, preorder, postorder, BFS, DFS), and graph representations (adjacency list, adjacency matrix). Vocabulary: data structure, array, linked list, tree, graph, node, edge, hash table, heap, BST, traversal, Big-O. Common errors: pointer/reference manipulation errors, not handling edge cases (empty structures), and choosing inefficient data structures.

Computer programming.teks_description: Computer Science II: Apply algorithm design strategies including divide and conquer, recursion, and efficiency
Computer programming.prompt: This topic covers algorithm design strategies for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.34.c.4). Students should apply divide and conquer, greedy algorithms, dynamic programming, and recursive strategies. Key concepts: Big-O notation (O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n^2), O(2^n)), analyzing time and space complexity, recurrence relations (Master Theorem), and comparing algorithmic approaches. Key algorithms: merge sort, quick sort, Dijkstra's, binary search. Vocabulary: algorithm, complexity, Big-O, divide and conquer, greedy, dynamic programming, recursion, memoization, optimization, brute force. Common errors: incorrect complexity analysis, not recognizing overlapping subproblems for DP, and stack overflow from deep recursion.

Computer programming.teks_description: Identify and use concepts of object-oriented design including encapsulation and abstraction
Computer programming.prompt: This topic covers OOP principles for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.33.c.6D). Students should master the four pillars: encapsulation (bundling data and methods, access control), abstraction (hiding implementation details), inheritance (creating class hierarchies), and polymorphism (method overriding, interfaces). Key concepts: classes and objects, constructors, getters/setters, superclass/subclass, method overriding, abstract classes, interfaces, composition vs. inheritance, and SOLID principles. Vocabulary: encapsulation, abstraction, inheritance, polymorphism, class, object, interface, abstract, override, composition. Common errors: overusing inheritance (prefer composition), breaking encapsulation, and tight coupling between classes.

Computer programming.teks_description: Computer Science II: Apply software design principles and common design patterns
Computer programming.prompt: This topic covers software design patterns for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.34.c.6). Students should recognize and apply common patterns. Creational: Singleton (one instance), Factory (object creation), Builder (complex construction). Structural: Adapter (interface compatibility), Decorator (dynamic behavior), Composite (tree structures). Behavioral: Observer (event notification), Strategy (interchangeable algorithms), Iterator (sequential access). Key concepts: when to apply each pattern, pattern intent and structure, and avoiding pattern overuse. Vocabulary: design pattern, Singleton, Factory, Observer, Strategy, Decorator, MVC, anti-pattern, refactoring. Common errors: forcing patterns where they don't fit, overengineering, and confusing similar patterns.

Computer programming.teks_description: CTE Computer Programming: Apply software development methodologies and engineering principles
Computer programming.prompt: This topic covers software development methodologies for Computer Programming students (TEKS §130.276.c.5). Students should understand Agile/Scrum, Waterfall, and iterative development methodologies. Key concepts: requirements gathering, system design, code reviews, documentation, technical debt, DRY/KISS/YAGNI principles, clean code practices, and software development lifecycle (SDLC). Vocabulary: Agile, Scrum, sprint, user story, requirement, specification, refactoring, technical debt, code review, CI/CD, deployment. Students should follow best practices for naming, commenting, and organizing code. Common errors: skipping requirements analysis, inadequate documentation, and accumulating technical debt.

Computer programming.teks_description: CTE Computer Programming: Create and manipulate data using database management systems
Computer programming.prompt: This topic covers database concepts for Computer Programming students (TEKS §130.276.c.7). Students should design relational databases and write SQL queries. Key SQL: SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, JOIN (INNER, LEFT, RIGHT), GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY, WHERE, and aggregate functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX). Key concepts: relational model, tables/rows/columns, primary and foreign keys, normalization (1NF, 2NF, 3NF), ER diagrams, and CRUD operations. Vocabulary: database, table, row, column, primary key, foreign key, join, query, normalization, SQL, CRUD, schema, index. Common errors: SQL injection vulnerabilities, denormalized designs, missing foreign key constraints, and Cartesian products from missing JOIN conditions.

Computer programming.teks_description: CTE Computer Programming: Develop web-based applications using markup and scripting languages
Computer programming.prompt: This topic covers web development technologies for Computer Programming students (TEKS §130.276.c.8). Students should create web pages using HTML (structure), CSS (styling), and JavaScript (interactivity). Key HTML: semantic tags, forms, tables, links, images. Key CSS: selectors, box model, flexbox, grid, responsive design. Key JS: DOM manipulation, event handlers, fetch API, and basic frameworks. Key concepts: separation of concerns, responsive design, accessibility, and client-server interaction. Vocabulary: HTML, CSS, JavaScript, DOM, selector, responsive, flexbox, grid, event, API, AJAX, framework. Common errors: inline styling (prefer CSS classes), not making sites responsive, accessibility issues, and XSS vulnerabilities.

Computer programming.teks_description: Demonstrate proper digital etiquette, responsible use of software and version management tools
Computer programming.prompt: This topic covers advanced Git usage for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.33.c.5C). Students should manage code collaboratively using Git and GitHub/GitLab. Key concepts: branching strategies (feature branches, Git Flow), merge vs. rebase, pull requests/code reviews, resolving conflicts, .gitignore, tags and releases, and collaboration workflows. Advanced: cherry-pick, stash, bisect, interactive staging, and hooks. Vocabulary: repository, branch, merge, rebase, pull request, conflict, stash, tag, release, fork, clone, remote, upstream. Common errors: committing to wrong branch, force-pushing to shared branches, large binary files in repo, and leaked credentials.

Computer programming.teks_description: Test program solutions with valid and invalid test data; §126.33.c.4(J) debug using error messages
Computer programming.prompt: This topic covers software testing for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.33.c.4I, §126.33.c.4J). Students should write and run unit tests, integration tests, and use debugging tools. Key concepts: test-driven development (TDD), unit testing frameworks (unittest, pytest), test coverage, boundary value analysis, equivalence partitioning, mocking, and systematic debugging with debuggers and logging. Vocabulary: unit test, integration test, TDD, assertion, mock, stub, coverage, regression test, edge case, boundary value. Students should test with valid, invalid, and edge-case data. Common errors: testing only happy paths, brittle tests, insufficient coverage, and testing implementation instead of behavior.

Computer programming.teks_description: Computer Science II: Apply software architecture and modular design principles
Computer programming.prompt: This topic covers software architecture principles for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.34.c.6). Students should understand and apply architectural patterns: MVC (Model-View-Controller), microservices, layered architecture, event-driven architecture, and client-server. Key concepts: separation of concerns, modularity, coupling and cohesion, scalability, maintainability, and choosing architecture based on requirements. Vocabulary: architecture, MVC, microservices, monolith, API, layer, module, coupling, cohesion, scalability, middleware. Common errors: monolithic design for complex systems, tight coupling between components, and overarchitecting simple applications.

Computer programming.teks_description: Identify and describe the function of major hardware components and operating system concepts
Computer programming.prompt: This topic covers OS fundamentals for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.33.c.6A). Students should understand how operating systems manage hardware and software resources. Key concepts: processes and threads, memory management (stack vs. heap, virtual memory), file systems, CPU scheduling, I/O management, and the role of the kernel. Vocabulary: operating system, process, thread, kernel, memory, file system, CPU scheduling, virtual memory, stack, heap, deadlock, context switch. Common errors: confusing processes and threads, memory leaks, and race conditions in concurrent programs.

Computer programming.teks_description: CTE Computer Programming: Understand networking concepts and protocols
Computer programming.prompt: This topic covers networking concepts for Computer Programming students (TEKS §130.276.c.9). Students should understand how computers communicate over networks. Key concepts: OSI and TCP/IP models, IP addressing (IPv4/IPv6), DNS, HTTP/HTTPS protocols, TCP vs. UDP, ports, sockets, RESTful APIs, and basic network security. Vocabulary: network, protocol, IP address, DNS, TCP, UDP, HTTP, HTTPS, port, socket, API, REST, packet, bandwidth, latency, firewall. Common errors: confusing TCP and UDP use cases, hardcoding IP addresses, ignoring HTTPS for security, and not handling network timeouts.

Computer programming.teks_description: Investigate privacy and security measures in computing
Computer programming.prompt: This topic covers cybersecurity fundamentals for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.33.c.5D). Students should understand common security threats and protective measures. Key concepts: authentication vs. authorization, encryption (symmetric/asymmetric), hashing (SHA, bcrypt for passwords), common vulnerabilities (SQL injection, XSS, CSRF), secure coding practices, HTTPS/TLS, and the principle of least privilege. Vocabulary: authentication, authorization, encryption, hashing, vulnerability, exploit, firewall, SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, TLS, certificate, two-factor authentication. Common errors: storing passwords in plaintext, SQL injection from unsanitized input, and security through obscurity.

Computer programming.teks_description: Computer Science III: Apply advanced programming concepts including concurrency
Computer programming.prompt: This topic covers concurrent programming for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.35.c.5). Students should understand threads, processes, and asynchronous programming. Key concepts: multithreading vs. multiprocessing, race conditions, deadlocks, mutexes/locks, semaphores, thread safety, Python's GIL, async/await pattern, and parallel algorithms. Vocabulary: concurrency, parallelism, thread, process, race condition, deadlock, mutex, semaphore, synchronization, async, await, GIL, thread pool. Common errors: race conditions from shared state, deadlocks from lock ordering, and not understanding Python's GIL limitation for CPU-bound tasks.

Computer programming.teks_description: Mobile Application Development TEKS: Design and develop mobile applications
Computer programming.prompt: This topic covers mobile application development for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.39). Students should understand mobile app architecture, UI/UX considerations for mobile, and development approaches. Key concepts: native vs. cross-platform development, responsive design for various screen sizes, touch interaction patterns, app lifecycle, local storage, API consumption, and app store deployment. Vocabulary: mobile app, native, cross-platform, responsive, UI, UX, lifecycle, API, SDK, emulator, deploy, React Native, Flutter, Swift, Kotlin. Common errors: not considering different screen sizes, poor touch target sizing, excessive battery/data usage, and ignoring platform design guidelines.

Computer programming.teks_description: CTE Computer Programming: Understand cloud computing concepts and services
Computer programming.prompt: This topic covers cloud computing concepts for Computer Programming students (TEKS §130.276.c.10). Students should understand cloud service models and deployment patterns. Key concepts: IaaS, PaaS, SaaS models, major providers (AWS, Azure, GCP), virtual machines vs. containers (Docker), serverless computing, cloud storage, databases as a service, CI/CD pipelines, and cost management. Vocabulary: cloud, IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, VM, container, Docker, serverless, AWS, Azure, GCP, scaling, load balancer, CDN, DevOps. Common errors: leaving resources running (cost overruns), poor security configuration, vendor lock-in, and not understanding shared responsibility model.

Computer programming.teks_description: Investigate computing and computing-related advancements including AI
Computer programming.prompt: This topic covers AI fundamentals for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.33.c.5E). Students should understand what AI is, its capabilities and limitations, and basic approaches. Key concepts: rule-based systems vs. machine learning, supervised/unsupervised/reinforcement learning, neural networks (high level), natural language processing, computer vision, AI ethics and bias, and practical AI tools and APIs. Vocabulary: artificial intelligence, machine learning, neural network, training data, model, prediction, classification, NLP, computer vision, bias, ethics, AGI, narrow AI. Common errors: overstating AI capabilities, ignoring bias in training data, and confusing AI with automation.

Computer programming.teks_description: Investigate computing advancements including machine learning and data science
Computer programming.prompt: This topic covers machine learning fundamentals for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.33.c.5E). Students should understand how ML models learn from data. Key concepts: supervised learning (regression, classification), unsupervised learning (clustering, dimensionality reduction), training/validation/test splits, overfitting vs. underfitting, feature engineering, and evaluation metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1). Key algorithms: linear regression, decision trees, k-nearest neighbors, k-means clustering. Vocabulary: machine learning, model, training, testing, feature, label, overfitting, underfitting, cross-validation, accuracy, precision, recall. Common errors: training on test data, not normalizing features, ignoring class imbalance, and overfitting to training data.

Computer programming.teks_description: Create interactive input interfaces with relevant user prompts and good design
Computer programming.prompt: This topic covers user interface and experience design for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.33.c.3B). Students should create intuitive, accessible, and visually appealing interfaces. Key concepts: usability heuristics (Nielsen's 10), visual hierarchy, consistency, feedback, accessibility (WCAG guidelines), color theory, typography, whitespace, responsive design, and user testing. Vocabulary: UI, UX, usability, accessibility, wireframe, prototype, heuristic, responsive, WCAG, contrast ratio, affordance, user flow, persona. Common errors: inconsistent interfaces, poor color contrast (accessibility), information overload, and designing for developers instead of users.

Computer programming.teks_description: Computer Science II: Apply programming interfaces and API integration patterns
Computer programming.prompt: This topic covers API design and integration for Computer Programming students (TEKS §126.34.c.4). Students should create and consume RESTful APIs, understand HTTP methods, and handle data formats. Key concepts: REST principles, HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH), status codes (200, 201, 400, 401, 404, 500), JSON data format, API authentication (API keys, OAuth, JWT), rate limiting, and API documentation. Vocabulary: API, REST, endpoint, HTTP method, status code, JSON, authentication, authorization, token, rate limit, CRUD, webhook. Common errors: not handling error responses, ignoring rate limits, insecure API key storage, and inconsistent endpoint naming.
